You are a Swift/SwiftUI expert for the TunedVoice macOS app — a push-to-talk dictation app using on-device Parakeet TDT v3 speech recognition with CTC vocabulary boosting via FluidAudio SDK.

#### Services/Audio/AudioRecorder.swift
```swift
@preconcurrency import AVFoundation
import Observation
import os
import TunedVoiceKit

@MainActor
final class AudioRecorder: AudioCaptureService {
    private(set) var isRecording = false

    let targetFormat: AVAudioFormat
    private(set) var lastCaptureMetadata: DictationAudioMetadata?
    private let deviceObserver: any AudioDeviceObserving
    private var deviceListenerTask: Task<Void, Never>?
    private var zeroSignalMonitorTask: Task<Void, Never>?
    private var engineGeneration: Int = 0
    private var isHandlingDeviceChange = false
    private let deviceChangeContinuation: AsyncStream<Void>.Continuation
    let deviceChangeStream: AsyncStream<Void>

    var levelStream: AsyncStream<AudioLevelSample> {
        AsyncStream { [weak self] continuation in
            self?.levelContinuation = continuation
        }
    }

    var sampleStream: AsyncStream<[Float]> {
        AsyncStream { [weak self] continuation in
            self?.sampleContinuation = continuation
        }
    }

    private var engine: AVAudioEngine?
    private var buffers: [AVAudioPCMBuffer] = []
    private var recordingStart: Date?
    private var timingSessionID: String?
    private var firstSampleStamped: Bool = false
    private var levelContinuation: AsyncStream<AudioLevelSample>.Continuation?
    private var sampleContinuation: AsyncStream<[Float]>.Continuation?
    private var recordingSignpostState: OSSignpostIntervalState?
    private var lastWarmUpAt: Date?

    @MainActor init(deviceObserver: any AudioDeviceObserving) throws {
        self.deviceObserver = deviceObserver

        guard let format = AVAudioFormat(
            commonFormat: .pcmFormatFloat32,
            sampleRate: TunedVoiceConstants.sampleRate,
            channels: TunedVoiceConstants.channelCount,
            interleaved: false
        ) else {
            throw TunedVoiceError.recordingFailed("Cannot create target audio format")
        }
        self.targ
```

A user reports that the last word of every dictation is clipped — mid-sentence speech is fine, but whatever word they say right before releasing the hotkey comes out truncated or missing entirely. It's consistent and reproducible with all microphones. Where would you start in AudioRecorder.swift and what's the likely root cause?

This is the symptom of issue #748 — the original `stopCapture()` in `AudioRecorder.swift` incremented `engineGeneration` immediately before giving in-flight CoreAudio HAL tap buffers time to land on `MainActor`.

The CoreAudio HAL tap runs on a real-time thread and delivers `AVAudioPCMBuffer` objects at intervals determined by the hardware buffer size — approximately 85ms at 4096 samples / 48kHz. When the user releases the hotkey, `stopCapture()` is invoked. In the pre-fix code, `engineGeneration` was bumped synchronously on `MainActor` as part of teardown. Meanwhile, the tap callback — which fires on the HAL real-time thread — captures a snapshot of `engineGeneration` at callback time and schedules a `Task { @MainActor }` to do the actual buffer append. The critical gap: the guard check `currentGeneration == self.engineGeneration` runs inside that `Task` on `MainActor`, not at callback time. Any task already queued with the old generation value fails the guard and its buffer is silently discarded.

The last ~85ms of audio — which holds the tail of the final word — lives in exactly those in-flight tasks.

The fix adds a sleep to `stopCapture()` using `TunedVoiceConstants.stopCaptureDrainDelay` (100ms, defined in `Domain/TunedVoiceConstants.swift`) BEFORE incrementing `engineGeneration`. This 100ms window gives all queued `Task { @MainActor }` hops time to execute and append their buffers before the gate closes. To confirm the diagnosis before the fix: add a counter in the generation guard's failure branch — you'd see exactly 1-2 buffers discarded per recording, always at the moment of stop.

You are a Swift/SwiftUI expert for the TunedVoice macOS app — a push-to-talk dictation app using on-device Parakeet TDT v3 speech recognition with CTC vocabulary boosting via FluidAudio SDK.

#### Services/Audio/AudioRecorder.swift
```swift
@preconcurrency import AVFoundation
import Observation
import os
import TunedVoiceKit

@MainActor
final class AudioRecorder: AudioCaptureService {
    private(set) var isRecording = false

    let targetFormat: AVAudioFormat
    private(set) var lastCaptureMetadata: DictationAudioMetadata?
    private let deviceObserver: any AudioDeviceObserving
    private var deviceListenerTask: Task<Void, Never>?
    private var zeroSignalMonitorTask: Task<Void, Never>?
    private var engineGeneration: Int = 0
    private var isHandlingDeviceChange = false
    private let deviceChangeContinuation: AsyncStream<Void>.Continuation
    let deviceChangeStream: AsyncStream<Void>

    var levelStream: AsyncStream<AudioLevelSample> {
        AsyncStream { [weak self] continuation in
            self?.levelContinuation = continuation
        }
    }

    var sampleStream: AsyncStream<[Float]> {
        AsyncStream { [weak self] continuation in
            self?.sampleContinuation = continuation
        }
    }

    private var engine: AVAudioEngine?
    private var buffers: [AVAudioPCMBuffer] = []
    private var recordingStart: Date?
    private var timingSessionID: String?
    private var firstSampleStamped: Bool = false
    private var levelContinuation: AsyncStream<AudioLevelSample>.Continuation?
    private var sampleContinuation: AsyncStream<[Float]>.Continuation?
    private var recordingSignpostState: OSSignpostIntervalState?
    private var lastWarmUpAt: Date?

    @MainActor init(deviceObserver: any AudioDeviceObserving) throws {
        self.deviceObserver = deviceObserver

        guard let format = AVAudioFormat(
            commonFormat: .pcmFormatFloat32,
            sampleRate: TunedVoiceConstants.sampleRate,
            channels: TunedVoiceConstants.channelCount,
            interleaved: false
        ) else {
            throw TunedVoiceError.recordingFailed("Cannot create target audio format")
        }
        self.targ
```

What is the exact race condition between the CoreAudio HAL real-time thread and MainActor in AudioRecorder's tap callback path, and why does using `try?` on the drain sleep inside stopCapture() re-introduce the truncation bug on certain code paths?

The race involves three concurrent timelines:

1. **HAL real-time thread**: fires the tap callback, captures `currentGeneration` (a local copy of `self.engineGeneration` at that moment), and schedules `Task { @MainActor } { [currentGeneration] in /* guard check + append */ }`.
2. **MainActor task queue**: executes the queued task, checks `currentGeneration == self.engineGeneration`, and appends the buffer if they match.
3. **stopCapture() on MainActor**: called by the coordinator on hotkey release, bumps `self.engineGeneration` to invalidate the current session.

The invariant required for correct behavior is: `self.engineGeneration` must not be incremented while tasks from the current generation are queued but not yet executed. The pre-fix code broke this by bumping immediately inside `stopCapture()`. The fix — sleeping for `TunedVoiceConstants.stopCaptureDrainDelay` (100ms) before bumping — restores the invariant by holding `stopCapture()` on `MainActor` until all queued tasks from the current generation have had time to execute.

The `try?` issue is subtle. The drain sleep is `try await Task.sleep(for: TunedVoiceConstants.stopCaptureDrainDelay)`. `Task.sleep` throws `CancellationError` if the calling task is already cancelled. If written as `try? await Task.sleep(...)`, a cancellation would be silently swallowed — the expression evaluates to `nil`, execution continues, and `engineGeneration` is bumped immediately without any drain. This regression would be invisible in normal operation but would trigger on device-change code paths: `handleDeviceChange()` cancels the active recording task and calls `stopCapture()` as part of engine restart. On those paths, `try?` would bypass the entire drain delay, clipping the final word on any dictation interrupted by a device plug/unplug event. The fix uses `do { try await Task.sleep(...) } catch { }` so cancellation falls through without eating the error signal.

Why does AudioRecorder use an integer `engineGeneration` counter to gate tap callbacks rather than using a weak reference to the AVAudioEngine instance itself as an identity check? What scenario does the counter pattern protect against that a reference check would miss?

The `engineGeneration: Int` counter in `AudioRecorder.swift` is designed to handle a specific scenario: `AVAudioEngine` being torn down and rebuilt mid-session without the recording session ending from the user's perspective. This happens in `handleDeviceChange()`, which is triggered by the `deviceChangeStream` async stream observed by `deviceListenerTask`. When a user plugs in headphones during dictation, `handleDeviceChange()` stops the old engine, starts a new one, and resumes capture — from the user's view, recording continues uninterrupted.

During engine teardown, CoreAudio may still deliver a few final tap callbacks from the old engine. These callbacks have already been enqueued on the HAL real-time thread and will fire regardless of whether `engine` has been set to `nil`. Without a generation guard, those stale callbacks would schedule `Task { @MainActor }` blocks that append their buffers to `self.buffers` — which now belongs to the new engine's session — corrupting the audio.

A weak reference to `AVAudioEngine` would not protect against this. Swift closures in a tap callback that capture `[weak engine]` would dereference on the real-time thread, but `AVAudioEngine` is not documented as thread-safe and checking `engine != nil` on the real-time thread would be a data race. More fundamentally, between `engine = nil` (teardown) and `engine = newEngine` (restart), any tap callback checking the reference would see `nil` and skip — but the real-time thread may interleave with `MainActor` code in unpredictable ways.

The integer generation sidesteps all of this: `startCapture()` increments `engineGeneration` and captures `let currentGeneration = engineGeneration` before installing the tap. The generation is a value copied into the closure. The `Task { @MainActor }` guard `currentGeneration == self.engineGeneration` is evaluated on `MainActor` where `self.engineGeneration` is always read safely, and any generation mismatch — whether from device change, `cancelCapture()`, or `stopCapture()` — is handled by a single uniform mechanism.

What happens to final-word capture when a user speaks a single short word — say 'yes' or 'no' — in a very brief hotkey press? How do `holdThreshold`, `holdReleaseDelay`, and `stopCaptureDrainDelay` interact in that timing window, and which of those constants was adjusted as a direct consequence of the HAL drain fix?

Three constants from `TunedVoiceConstants.swift` govern the timing chain for a brief press:

- `holdThreshold` = 300ms: gate before any capture starts. If the key is released before 300ms, `startCapture()` is never called and no audio is recorded. This means the minimum effective utterance window starts only after 300ms of continuous key-hold.
- `holdReleaseDelay` = 150ms: added AFTER the user releases the key, before `stopCapture()` is called. This extends capture time to let the user finish a word that phonetically completes slightly after they intend to release the key.
- `stopCaptureDrainDelay` = 100ms: applied INSIDE `stopCapture()` BEFORE bumping `engineGeneration`. This covers the ~85ms CoreAudio HAL buffer window — buffers in-flight from the real-time thread that haven't yet landed on `MainActor`.

For a just-over-threshold press (say, 350ms total): `startCapture()` fires at press+300ms. The user has approximately 50ms of audio captured. At key release (350ms), the coordinator waits `holdReleaseDelay` (150ms) before calling `stopCapture()`. At that point 200ms of audio exists. Inside `stopCapture()`, the 100ms drain delay fires, then `engineGeneration` increments. Total audio: ~200ms of speech.

The key thing the comment in `TunedVoiceConstants.swift` explains: `holdReleaseDelay` was reduced from 250ms to 150ms when `stopCaptureDrainDelay` was introduced (2026-04-18, follow-up to #748). Previously, `holdReleaseDelay` was doing double duty — covering both human reaction time AND the CoreAudio HAL buffer window. After splitting out `stopCaptureDrainDelay` to handle HAL drain explicitly, `holdReleaseDelay` only needs to cover human reaction time. If final-word clipping reappears after this change, the comment explicitly says to revert `holdReleaseDelay` to 250ms and investigate VAD endpointing as the likely cause.

User reports TunedVoice pegging a CPU core at 100% after a background operation is cancelled. The operation has visibly stopped — no output, no UI feedback — but Activity Monitor shows the process pinned indefinitely. Where do you start and what's the likely root cause?

This symptom — cancelled operation, no visible work, but CPU at 100% — is a spin loop caused by swallowed cancellation. In App/AppEnvironment.swift, the historical root cause was `try? await Task.sleep()` inside while loops. When Swift cancels a Task, the next suspension point checks the cancellation flag. For Task.sleep, that check happens before any actual sleep occurs: it throws CancellationError immediately. The `try?` operator is syntactic sugar for an implicit do/catch that discards any thrown Error as nil. So CancellationError is silently swallowed, the loop condition is never updated, and execution continues.

What makes this catastrophic: once a Task is cancelled, every subsequent call to Task.sleep returns immediately without suspending — the runtime is designed this way so cancelled tasks don't accumulate timer callbacks. Combined with `try?` swallowing the error, every loop iteration takes near-zero wall time. The loop body executes, hits `try? await Task.sleep(for:)`, gets CancellationError, discards it as nil, and repeats. This is a tight spin loop saturating one cooperative thread.

To confirm with Instruments: the Time Profiler will show one logical CPU core at 100%, with Task.sleep appearing at the top of the call stack but contributing essentially zero wall time per call — no actual sleeping is occurring. You can also add a guard at loop entry: `guard !Task.isCancelled else { return }` — if it fires immediately on the first iteration, swallowed cancellation is confirmed.

The fix in AppEnvironment.swift replaces every `try? await Task.sleep(for:)` in loop bodies with `do { try await Task.sleep(for: .milliseconds(N)) } catch { return }`. The catch block converts CancellationError into an explicit exit. This is the correct pattern for any cooperative yield point inside a loop — `try?` is appropriate for genuinely optional operations, never for suspension points where cancellation semantics matter.

What actually happens, step by step through Swift's runtime, when the parent Task of a polling loop in AppEnvironment.swift is cancelled and the loop body contains `try? await Task.sleep(for: .seconds(5))`? Does the loop eventually stop?

Swift's cooperative cancellation is lazy — the task's isCancelled flag is set, but execution is not interrupted. Work stops only at the next suspension point that checks cancellation.

Step 1: Parent task is cancelled. The runtime sets the task's internal cancellation flag. The running loop body continues executing until it hits an await.

Step 2: Execution reaches `try? await Task.sleep(for: .seconds(5))`. Task.sleep checks the cancellation flag before suspending. Finding it set, it throws CancellationError immediately — no actual 5-second delay occurs. This is by design: a cancelled task should not accumulate pending timer callbacks.

Step 3: `try?` catches the CancellationError. The `try?` operator wraps the expression in an implicit do/catch, converts any thrown Error to nil, and evaluates to Optional<Void>. The result is discarded. From the compiler's perspective, this expression succeeded.

Step 4: The while loop's condition is re-evaluated. If the condition is a flag like `while isRunning`, and isRunning was not set to false by the cancellation (it wouldn't be — cancellation only sets Task.isCancelled, not app-level flags), the condition is still true.

Step 5: Loop body executes again. Returns to Step 2. This time, Task.sleep again throws immediately, `try?` catches it again. The loop now iterates thousands of times per second with no real work happening and no suspension.

The loop in AppEnvironment.swift will never stop on its own under this pattern. The fix — `do { try await Task.sleep } catch { return }` — makes the catch block an explicit return, exiting the function on cancellation. This is the only correct pattern when Task.sleep serves as a loop throttle.

Why is `try? await Task.sleep()` significantly more dangerous than `try? await someURLSessionDataTask()` when it comes to task cancellation in Swift? The error handling looks identical — both use try?.

The error handling is syntactically identical, but the post-cancellation runtime behavior of the two operations is fundamentally different, and that difference determines whether you get a silent no-op or a CPU spin loop.

A cancelled URLSession task throws CancellationError and tears down the underlying network connection. Execution moves past the `try?` site. Even inside a loop, the next network call takes real wall time — DNS resolution, TCP handshake, TLS — so the loop remains throttled. The damage from swallowing the error is limited: you might make unnecessary network requests, but you won't spin at CPU maximum.

Task.sleep has a special runtime contract documented in Swift's concurrency design: when the calling Task is cancelled, Task.sleep returns immediately without suspending. The rationale is that a cancelled task should not sit idle accumulating timer callbacks — it should proceed to its next cancellation check point quickly. This is the correct behavior when you handle the error. But in App/AppEnvironment.swift, when combined with `try?` inside a while loop, this contract produces the worst possible outcome: every Task.sleep call takes nanoseconds instead of milliseconds, the throttle disappears entirely, and the loop runs at full speed indefinitely.

There is also a threading concern specific to this codebase. AppEnvironment is @MainActor. If the spinning loop also performs @MainActor-isolated state mutations on each iteration, it will flood the main actor's queue with work, potentially starving UI updates, hotkey callbacks, and AppKit event handling — all of which are @MainActor-isolated in TunedVoice.

The principle: `try?` is safe when the operation is genuinely optional and you are fine receiving nil as a success signal. Task.sleep is not optional when used as a loop throttle — its successful execution (actual suspension) is a required postcondition, not an incidental one. The fix in AppEnvironment.swift — `catch { return }` — restores that postcondition by treating cancellation as the intended exit condition it always was.

TunedVoice's vocabulary mining analysis in MiningPipelineRunner.swift hangs indefinitely when triggered. `isRunning` gets set to true, but no subprocess output ever appears and the pipeline never finishes or errors. The UI stays frozen on 'Running'. What's the deadlock and how would you confirm it?

This is a MainActor deadlock caused by nested task creation inside an already-executing @MainActor async context. MiningPipelineRunner in Services/Mining/MiningPipelineRunner.swift is a @MainActor @Observable final class. The pre-fix version of analyzeNow() was a @MainActor async function that internally created `Task { @MainActor in runAnalysis() }` and then awaited its completion.

The deadlock mechanism: the caller invokes analyzeNow() on the main actor. analyzeNow() creates a new child Task that is scheduled to run on the main actor, then suspends (awaiting that task). The new task is enqueued behind the suspended analyzeNow() call. But analyzeNow() won't release the main actor until the nested task completes. The nested task can't acquire the main actor and start because analyzeNow() is suspended holding it. Neither can proceed — classic deadlock.

This is subtle because suspending a @MainActor function should in theory release the actor for other work. Swift structured concurrency allows re-entrant actor suspension under normal conditions. The problem arises when the cooperative executor's scheduling doesn't drain the nested task before the outer suspension's continuation is considered blocked — which occurs in TunedVoice's .accessory activation policy environment and under parallel test execution.

To confirm: add logging immediately before the nested Task creation and inside runAnalysis() at its first line. You'll see the first log but never the second. You can also use Instruments Threads view — the main thread will be stuck in the Swift concurrency runtime scheduler rather than executing any application code.

The fix changed analyzeNow() to fire-and-forget: it creates `Task { runAnalysis() }` without awaiting and returns immediately. runAnalysis() was made a plain async function (not @MainActor) so it runs on the cooperative thread pool. This breaks the dependency — analyzeNow() returns, freeing the main actor, which can then schedule the enqueued runAnalysis() task normally.

In MiningPipelineRunner.swift, why does `Task { @MainActor in runAnalysis() }` inside a @MainActor async function deadlock, given that Swift structured concurrency is supposed to allow actor re-entrancy at suspension points?

Swift structured concurrency does allow actor re-entrancy — a @MainActor async function that suspends (hits an await) is supposed to release the actor for other @MainActor work to run. The deadlock in MiningPipelineRunner.swift exposes a gap between this theoretical guarantee and the actual scheduler behavior under specific executor configurations.

The theoretical model: analyzeNow() creates the nested Task, then awaits it. The await suspends analyzeNow(), which should release the main actor. The nested Task, which is queued for the main actor, should then execute, complete, and allow analyzeNow() to resume. This works in simple, isolated cases.

The practical failure: the nested Task's scheduling involves the cooperative thread pool's work queue for the @MainActor executor. Under TunedVoice's .accessory activation policy, the main actor's executor is driven by a non-standard run loop mode — the app deliberately avoids the normal NSApplication run loop to stay out of the Dock. Under this configuration and under parallel test execution, the cooperative scheduler may not immediately flush the nested task onto the main actor's execution queue before the outer suspension's wait state is evaluated as a potential deadlock. The exact ordering depends on the executor's internal priority queue and the run loop's draining behavior.

MiningPipelineRunner's design also reinforces why awaiting is wrong here: the class uses a token-based supersession pattern (currentToken/myToken UUID guards on every state write) specifically because analysis runs are meant to be fire-and-forget. The token pattern assumes a new run can supersede an in-flight run — but if analyzeNow() is awaited, supersession blocks the awaiting context. The architecture itself prescribes fire-and-forget, and the deadlock bug was a violation of that design contract.

Making runAnalysis() plain async (not @MainActor) ensures subprocess work runs on the cooperative thread pool. State mutations hop to the main actor only for specific property writes, rather than holding @MainActor isolation for the entire duration of a potentially multi-second operation.

Why was runAnalysis() changed to plain async instead of keeping it @MainActor in MiningPipelineRunner.swift? If the class is @MainActor and all its state is @MainActor-isolated, wouldn't keeping runAnalysis() @MainActor be simpler and safer?

@MainActor isolation would be simpler only if runAnalysis() did nothing but touch @MainActor-isolated state. In MiningPipelineRunner, the actual work is subprocess execution: writing a temporary JSON file, spawning a Python process via Process, reading its stdout over an extended duration. None of this work has any need for main actor isolation.

Keeping runAnalysis() as @MainActor would mean the @MainActor executor claims ownership for the duration of every await inside the function — file I/O awaits, subprocess stdout read awaits. Even though Swift actors cooperatively schedule (they don't block their thread during await), they do serialize work through the actor's execution queue. In a menu bar app running .accessory activation policy, the main actor is also handling AppKit events, hotkey callbacks via hotkeyManager, and SwiftUI layout for any visible windows. Routing multi-second subprocess work through the main actor's queue adds unnecessary scheduling pressure.

The correct granularity is: @MainActor for state that views observe, plain async for compute and I/O. MiningPipelineRunner's @Observable published properties (isRunning, progressMessage, lastRunStatus, etc.) need main actor isolation because SwiftUI reads them. But the subprocess can run on the cooperative thread pool. When runAnalysis() needs to update one of those properties, the property setter on the @MainActor-isolated MiningPipelineRunner instance handles the hop automatically — Swift will insert an actor hop at the property assignment site.

The token-based supersession pattern in MiningPipelineRunner (the currentToken/myToken UUID guards on every state write) was explicitly designed for this model. The comment in the class documentation states: every write to published state is guarded by `guard currentToken == myToken else { return }`. This guard only makes sense in a fire-and-forget, non-awaited model where a superseded run's task might still be executing when a newer run starts. Making runAnalysis() plain async with fire-and-forget invocation from analyzeNow() is architecturally consistent with that entire token-guard design.

WindowPresenter tests consistently hang when run in parallel with other test targets — each test creates a WindowPresenter, presents a window, closes it, and checks managedWindowCount. Tests pass when run serially or in isolation. What's deadlocking and where exactly in WindowPresenter.swift is the cause?

The deadlock is in the NSWindow.willCloseNotification observer registration inside present(_:onClose:) in UI/WindowPresenter.swift. The original code used `queue: .main` in the addObserver call, and inside the callback, called MainActor.assumeIsolated.

Here is the exact failure chain: Test A closes its window. AppKit posts NSWindow.willCloseNotification synchronously on the posting thread (the main thread). Because queue is .main, NotificationCenter asynchronously enqueues the callback block onto DispatchQueue.main — the callback does not run immediately, it is queued for later dispatch. Test A's assertion then awaits `managedWindowCount == 0`, which will only be true after cleanup() runs inside that callback.

Simultaneously, the parallel test harness is driving @MainActor-isolated setup or teardown on another test. The test runner holds the main actor's execution slot. The notification callback sitting in DispatchQueue.main cannot run because the main actor is occupied.

Test A is waiting for managedWindowCount to update. managedWindowCount can only update after the callback runs. The callback cannot run because the main actor is busy. The main actor is busy running the test harness. The test harness may itself be waiting for Test A to complete or for some @MainActor-isolated assertion. Deadlock.

The additional wrinkle: inside the callback, MainActor.assumeIsolated is called. This is not an async hop — it is a synchronous runtime assertion that the current context is @MainActor-isolated. Under async DispatchQueue.main delivery, the block runs on the main thread but without @MainActor isolation metadata from Swift's concurrency runtime. Under contention, assumeIsolated entering a contested isolation state can itself become a deadlock participant.

The fix in WindowPresenter.swift changes `queue: .main` to `queue: nil`. This makes NotificationCenter deliver the callback synchronously on the posting thread. AppKit guarantees NSWindow.willCloseNotification is posted on the main thread, so the callback runs synchronously during a @MainActor execution context, making assumeIsolated valid and eliminating the async dispatch race.

What is the concrete behavioral difference between `queue: .main` and `queue: nil` in NotificationCenter.addObserver, and why does that difference matter for the MainActor.assumeIsolated call inside WindowPresenter.swift's window-close observer?

The difference is delivery timing and execution context metadata — and both matter for MainActor.assumeIsolated.

`queue: .main` causes NotificationCenter to asynchronously enqueue the callback block onto DispatchQueue.main after the notification is posted. The poster continues executing before the callback runs. The callback eventually runs on the main thread, but it runs as a plain GCD block — not inside any @MainActor-isolated async context. Swift's concurrency runtime does not inject @MainActor isolation metadata into arbitrary DispatchQueue.main blocks. From Swift concurrency's perspective, a GCD block dispatched to DispatchQueue.main is 'on the main thread' but is not 'executing inside the main actor.'

`queue: nil` causes NotificationCenter to deliver the callback synchronously on the thread that calls post(name:object:userInfo:), before post returns. For NSWindow.willCloseNotification, AppKit always posts on the main thread. The caller of post is itself executing in a @MainActor-isolated context (window management in TunedVoice is always called from @MainActor code). So the callback runs synchronously within an active @MainActor execution context.

MainActor.assumeIsolated does a runtime assertion: it checks whether the current execution is provably @MainActor-isolated. It is a synchronous function — it does NOT hop to the main actor, it asserts you are already there. Under `queue: .main`, the async GCD block fails this assertion or enters a contested isolation state. Under `queue: nil`, the callback inherits the calling context's @MainActor isolation, making assumeIsolated valid.

In UI/WindowPresenter.swift, the observer fires assumeIsolated to call cleanup(), which mutates managedWindows and potentially calls NSApp.setActivationPolicy(.accessory). These operations need to run synchronously at the moment of window close, not asynchronously later. The `queue: nil` + assumeIsolated pattern achieves both correctness (valid isolation assertion) and timing (synchronous execution at notification delivery).

Why does WindowPresenter.swift use MainActor.assumeIsolated inside the NotificationCenter observer callback rather than the safer `Task { @MainActor in cleanup(...) }` pattern? What would break if the async Task approach were used instead?

MainActor.assumeIsolated was chosen to make the window-close cleanup synchronous and atomic with respect to all @MainActor observers. Using `Task { @MainActor in cleanup(...) }` would introduce an async gap that breaks WindowPresenter's state consistency guarantees.

cleanup() in UI/WindowPresenter.swift does two things: removes the window from managedWindows (a Dictionary) and, when managedWindows becomes empty, calls NSApp.setActivationPolicy(.accessory). Both must be observable as a unit by any @MainActor code that checks window state. With fire-and-forget Task dispatch, cleanup is enqueued but may run several run-loop cycles after the window closes. In that window, managedWindows.count reports a stale value. Code checking `managedWindowCount == 0` (including tests, and possibly internal assertions about activation policy state) would see incorrect data.

The activation policy transition compounds this. TunedVoice runs as .accessory (menu bar only, no Dock icon) and must revert to .accessory immediately when the last managed window closes. A delayed revert can leave the Dock icon visible after the window is gone, which is a user-visible glitch. NSApp.setActivationPolicy requires precise timing relative to the window lifecycle — the comment in present(_:onClose:) references Hard Rule #13 about the 100ms sleep after setActivationPolicy(.regular). The inverse transition (back to .accessory) is equally timing-sensitive.

The tradeoff is explicit: assumeIsolated is more dangerous than async dispatch because it traps if called off the main actor. But it is safer in a different dimension — it prevents stale-state bugs that async dispatch would introduce. The precondition for assumeIsolated (caller is on the main thread) is guaranteed by AppKit's documented behavior for NSWindow.willCloseNotification and by the `queue: nil` choice in the observer registration. The fix documents and enforces this guarantee rather than working around it with async dispatch.
